content.text: Every transformer forward pass computes key and value tensors for each attention head at every layer. During autoregressive generation, the model processes one new token at a time — but the keys and values for all previous tokens are needed again for the next attention computation. The KV cache stores these tensors so they are computed once and reused, turning what would be O(n) repeated work per step into O(1). Without a KV cache, generating a 1000-token response requires computing attention over the full context 1000 times; with a cache, each step needs only one new forward pass.
For each transformer layer and each attention head, the projection matrices
are applied to each token's hidden state to produce its key and value vectors. During generation these tensors are appended to the cache:

attrs.latex: \text{cache}[l][\text{step}] = \left( K_l^{(t)},\; V_l^{(t)} \right) = \left( h^{(t)} W_K^{(l)},\; h^{(t)} W_V^{(l)} \right)

content.text: , the attention computation for the new query
uses all cached keys and values concatenated with the new key/value pair — so the output correctly attends over the entire context without re-computing any prior activations.

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F


class CachedMultiHeadAttention(torch.nn.Module):
    """
    Minimal single-layer multi-head attention that maintains a KV cache.
    """
    def __init__(self, d_model: int, num_heads: int):
        super().__init__()
        assert d_model % num_heads == 0
        self.d_model = d_model
        self.num_heads = num_heads
        self.d_k = d_model // num_heads

        self.W_q = torch.nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_k = torch.nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_v = torch.nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_o = torch.nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)

        # KV cache: list of (key, value) tensors, one entry per decoded step
        self._k_cache: list[torch.Tensor] = []
        self._v_cache: list[torch.Tensor] = []

    def reset_cache(self) -> None:
        self._k_cache.clear()
        self._v_cache.clear()

    def forward(
        self,
        x: torch.Tensor,   # (batch, seq_len, d_model)
        use_cache: bool = True,
    ) -> torch.Tensor:
        B, T, _ = x.shape
        H, d_k = self.num_heads, self.d_k

        # Project to Q, K, V for the new tokens only
        Q = self.W_q(x).view(B, T, H, d_k).transpose(1, 2)   # (B, H, T, d_k)
        K = self.W_k(x).view(B, T, H, d_k).transpose(1, 2)
        V = self.W_v(x).view(B, T, H, d_k).transpose(1, 2)

        if use_cache:
            # Append new keys/values to the cache, then attend over ALL cached
            self._k_cache.append(K)
            self._v_cache.append(V)
            K_full = torch.cat(self._k_cache, dim=2)   # (B, H, total_len, d_k)
            V_full = torch.cat(self._v_cache, dim=2)
        else:
            K_full, V_full = K, V

        # Scaled dot-product attention
        scale = d_k ** -0.5
        attn = torch.matmul(Q, K_full.transpose(-2, -1)) * scale
        attn = F.softmax(attn, dim=-1)
        out = torch.matmul(attn, V_full)               # (B, H, T, d_k)

        # Reshape and project
        out = out.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, T, self.d_model)
        return self.W_o(out)


# Demonstrate reuse: same module, incremental tokens
attn = CachedMultiHeadAttention(d_model=128, num_heads=4)

# Prefill phase: process the prompt in one forward pass
prompt = torch.randn(1, 10, 128)
_ = attn(prompt, use_cache=True)
print(f"After prefill: {len(attn._k_cache)} cached block(s), total keys = 10")

# Decode phase: one token at a time, each re-using cached context
for step in range(5):
    new_token = torch.randn(1, 1, 128)
    out = attn(new_token, use_cache=True)
    total = sum(k.shape[2] for k in attn._k_cache)
    print(f"  Step {step}: output shape {out.shape}, total context length = {total}")

import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM

tok   = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2").eval()

prompt_ids = tok.encode("The transformer architecture", return_tensors="pt")

# --- Without cache: recomputes all keys and values every step ---
import time

with torch.no_grad():
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    # use_cache=False: full context re-processed at every decode step
    out_no_cache = model.generate(
        prompt_ids, max_new_tokens=50, do_sample=False, use_cache=False
    )
    t_no_cache = time.perf_counter() - t0

# --- With cache (default): compute K/V once, reuse ---
with torch.no_grad():
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    out_cached = model.generate(
        prompt_ids, max_new_tokens=50, do_sample=False, use_cache=True
    )
    t_cached = time.perf_counter() - t0

print(f"No cache:    {t_no_cache:.3f}s")
print(f"With cache:  {t_cached:.3f}s")
print(f"Speedup:     {t_no_cache / t_cached:.1f}×")
print(f"Outputs match: {(out_no_cache == out_cached).all().item()}")

# --- Inspect the returned past_key_values structure ---
with torch.no_grad():
    output = model(
        prompt_ids,
        use_cache=True,
        return_dict=True,
    )

pkv = output.past_key_values     # tuple of length num_layers
k, v = pkv[0]                    # first layer: (key, value)
print(f"\nModel has {len(pkv)} layers")
print(f"Layer 0 key:   shape = {k.shape}")   # (batch, num_heads, seq_len, head_dim)
print(f"Layer 0 value: shape = {v.shape}")
Populate cache with K/V for every prompt token
Parallel; O(n²) attention over prompt; GPU-bound
Append new K/V; attend over cache (no recompute)

The decode phase is memory-bandwidth-bound rather than compute-bound: the GPU spends most of its time reading the growing KV cache from HBM, not executing matrix multiplications. This is why techniques like Multi-Query Attention (MQA) and Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) — which reduce the number of K/V heads — improve decode throughput substantially without hurting model quality much.

import torch

d_model  = 4096
n_heads  = 32
d_k      = d_model // n_heads   # 128
seq_len  = 4096
batch    = 1

# MHA: one K/V head per Q head
kv_heads_mha = n_heads   # 32
# GQA (as in LLaMA 3 8B): 8 K/V heads shared across 32 Q heads
kv_heads_gqa = 8
# MQA: a single K/V head for all Q heads
kv_heads_mqa = 1

def kv_cache_bytes(kv_heads: int, seq_len: int, d_k: int,
                   n_layers: int = 32, dtype_bytes: int = 2) -> float:
    """Total KV cache memory in GB for a given number of KV heads."""
    # 2 tensors (K and V), n_layers layers, kv_heads heads
    total = 2 * n_layers * kv_heads * seq_len * d_k * dtype_bytes
    return total / (1024 ** 3)

print(f"MHA  ({kv_heads_mha} KV heads): {kv_cache_bytes(kv_heads_mha, seq_len, d_k):.2f} GB")
print(f"GQA  ({kv_heads_gqa} KV heads): {kv_cache_bytes(kv_heads_gqa, seq_len, d_k):.2f} GB")
print(f"MQA  ({kv_heads_mqa} KV head):  {kv_cache_bytes(kv_heads_mqa, seq_len, d_k):.2f} GB")

# MHA  (32 KV heads): 2.00 GB
# GQA  (8 KV heads):  0.50 GB
# MQA  (1 KV head):   0.06 GB
# GQA achieves 4× memory savings with negligible quality loss vs MHA
A local KV cache is valid only for one generation session — it is discarded between requests. Some hosted APIs extend this idea across requests with
: the provider stores the K/V tensors for a prefix that appears in many requests (e.g. a long system prompt) and reuses them across calls, charging reduced input-token prices for the cached prefix.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# A long system prompt that is shared across many user requests
LONG_SYSTEM = """You are a senior software architect with 20 years of experience.
""" + ("You have deep expertise in distributed systems. " * 500)   # ~2000 tokens

# First call: cache miss — full prompt tokens are charged
response1 = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens=100,
    system=[
        {
            "type": "text",
            "text": LONG_SYSTEM,
            "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"},   # mark prefix as cacheable
        }
    ],
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is a microservice?"}],
)
usage1 = response1.usage
print(f"First call  — input: {usage1.input_tokens}, cache_write: {getattr(usage1, 'cache_creation_input_tokens', 0)}")

# Second call: cache hit — prefix tokens are read from cache (5× cheaper)
response2 = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens=100,
    system=[
        {
            "type": "text",
            "text": LONG_SYSTEM,
            "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"},
        }
    ],
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is event sourcing?"}],
)
usage2 = response2.usage
print(f"Second call — input: {usage2.input_tokens}, cache_read: {getattr(usage2, 'cache_read_input_tokens', 0)}")
# cache_read tokens are billed at 10% of normal input token price

The KV cache is the single most impactful optimisation in production LLM serving. Without it, autoregressive generation scales as O(n²) per output token; with it, each decode step is O(n) in memory reads and O(1) in new computation. The tradeoff is GPU memory: every token in context consumes cache storage at every layer, and at long context lengths this can rival the model weights themselves. This memory pressure is precisely why paged attention, quantised caches, and grouped-query attention were developed — they all reduce cache size without eliminating it.
